topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office door always seemed to creak just a little louder today, a deliberate, drawn-out sound that scraped against my nerves. I kept glancing at the clock on the wall, the second hand ticking with an unnerving slowness. Professor Albright was packing up his desk, his movements unusually swift, almost frantic. Every rustle of paper, every click of a pen cap, sounded like a coded message directed solely at me. I re-read my outline for the tenth time, tracing the headings with my finger, searching for some hidden signal, some indication that this departure was more than just a change in faculty. The air in the hallway felt thick, heavy, as if someone was holding their breath just out of sight. When he finally gathered his briefcase, he didn't look at me. He just walked toward the exit, and I couldn't stop the cold knot tightening in my stomach.

[story 2]

"Are you sure you locked the window?" I asked my roommate, my voice sharper than intended. I didn't wait for an answer, just spun around, my eyes darting toward the darkened window overlooking the quad. It was late, and the silence of the dorm felt vast and expectant. I checked the lock three times, the metal cold under my frantic touch. Albright had mentioned leaving next week, and the implication of that sudden void in my academic structure was making my chest feel tight. I started rearranging my books on the shelf, pushing them closer together, creating a small, solid barrier against whatever unseen forces were gathering in the shadows of my room. Every shadow seemed to shift, morphing into a shape that wasn't quite right, and I kept my back to the door, listening intently for any sound that wasn't my own heartbeat thumping too loudly in my ears.

[story 3]

I spent the entire afternoon in the library stacks, pretending to study advanced calculus while my attention was entirely focused on the faculty bulletin board. The announcement about Professor Albright’s impending resignation seemed to vibrate in the silence of the reading room. I watched students pass by, their casual conversations sounding like static interference. I noticed a student lingering near the board, staring intently at the notice, and I felt a sudden, intense urge to move, to disappear into the towering shelves of reference texts. My hands were clammy, and I kept wiping them on my jeans, as if they were contaminated. I imagined that the papers Albright had graded were somehow altered, that the research he guided me through was being subtly undermined before he even left. The certainty that something was fundamentally wrong, something intentionally orchestrated, settled deep in my bones, making every shadow feel like an observer.
